attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with acute uncomplicated appendicitis. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. A laparoscopic appendicectomy under general anaesthesia is performed.

During the procedure his blood pressure decreases from 119/67 to 79/50 mmHg after administration of a propofol bolus. During this time there was minimal blood loss and no changes in intra-operative medications. His pre-operative blood pressure was 125/80 mmHg. On examination, his pulse rate is 97/min, blood pressure 79/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 12/min, SpO2 100%, and temperature 36.6°C.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
attributes.explanation:
Hypotensive episodes can often occur as a result of anaesthetic agents. Propofol can induce hypotension through its inhibition of the sympathetic nervous system and the baroreceptor reflexes. Hypotensive episodes should be treated promptly, where systolic blood pressure should be maintained within 20% of patients baseline (125*0.8 = 100mmHg in this patient) and/or above 100mmHg. The choice of agent depends on patient factors, such as the function of other organs, as well the degree of hypotension. In this patient, blood pressure is decreased whilst heart rate is borderline elevated. Increased heart rate is a response to decreased blood pressure in order to maintain cardiac output (cardiac output = stroke volume x heart rate). Baroreceptor mediated changes to circulatory vital signs such as heart rate are less marked under anaesthesia due to the relative suppression of the baroreceptor mechanism. As a result, increasing blood pressure in isolation is the ideal initial intra-operative response to attempt to return the patient to their prior stable state.

Metaraminol is the most appropriate medication to administer in this patient. It is a selective alpha 1-adrenergic agonist with minimal beta-adrenergic activity. As a result, it will cause vasoconstriction with minimal increase in heart rate. In this patient, heart rate is already elevated therefore isolated vasoconstriction is the most appropriate intervention. Phenylephrine is a medication very similar to metaraminol used widely in the USA, however in Australia metaraminol is more commonly used.

Reverse trendelenburg is the incorrect option as it positions the patient in a head up, feet down position, which would encourage blood to drain toward the feet, reducing venous return and therefore blood pressure. The (regular) trendelenburg position of feet up, head down, can be effective in temporarily increasing blood pressure in fluid-responsive patients as gravity increases venous return.

Adrenaline has both alpha and beta-adrenergic action, meaning it will increase heart rate as well as blood pressure. As an initial treatment, this is inappropriate for this patient due to their already elevated heart rate. It may be given as a second or third-line medication if metaraminol is ineffective.

Atropine is an antagonist of muscarinic receptors, decreasing vagal nerve to the heart thereby increasing heart rate. It has no vasoactive effects, therefore would not correct the hypotension in this patient.

Vasopressin acts to increase blood pressure through its anti-diuretic hormone activity in the long-term, as well as stimulating the nonadrenergic V1 receptor to cause vasoconstriction in the short term. However, it is usually reserved for treatment-refractory hypotension (i.e. if catecholamine or sympathomimetic medications fail). It may also cause skin necrosis if extravasation occurs. As a result, it is not the appropriate initial treatment for this patient.

As a note, noradrenaline may also be considered in this patient. It is also a selective alpha 1-adrenergic agonist with minimal beta-adrenergic activity, and is effective as an intra-operative solution to hypotension. However, it is rarely given as a bolus due to the risks from peripheral IV extravasation, instead usually being given as an infusion through a central line. If given as a bolus, it must be given in small doses in a very dilute solution.
learningPoints: Propofol can cause hypotension through inhibition of the sympathetic nervous system and baroreceptor reflexes.
Metaraminol is an appropriate first-line agent for intraoperative hypotension as it causes vasoconstriction with minimal increase in heart rate.
Intraoperative blood pressure should be maintained within 20% of the patient's baseline or above 100 mmHg.
references: Tsikas D, Jordan J, Engeli S. Blood pressure-lowering effects of propofol or sevoflurane anaesthesia are not due to enhanced nitric oxide formation or bioavailability. British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology. 2015;79(6):1030–3. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4456136/

London MJ. Hemodynamic management during anesthesia in adults [Internet]. Joshi GP, Mark JB, Nussmeier NA, editors. UpToDate. 2022 [cited 2023Mar16]. Available from: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/hemodynamic-management-during-anesthesia-in-adults#!
Metaraminol [Internet]. Safer Care Victoria. 2018 [cited 2023Mar16]. Available from: https://www.safercare.vic.gov.au/clinical-guidance/critical/metaraminol

McLendon K, Preuss CV. Atropine. [Updated 2022 Sep 21]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470551/
